risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity (29). Groups were stratified according to baseline six minute walk test with a threshold of 300 meters and baseline upright forced vital capacity with a threshold of 55% of the predicted value." Comment: The paper explicitly states that a minimization algorithm was used, which is listed as an acceptable random sequence generation method. Although stratification is mentioned, the sequence generation method itself is described sufficiently to judge the allocation sequence as random/adequately generated.

Quote: "Ninety patients were randomized 2:1 to the treatment arm using a minimization algorithm in an effort to balance the groups for disease severity." Comment: The paper reports the randomization method and blinding, but it does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk from the available information.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study was a post-hoc analysis of cardiovascular parameters in a double-blind, multi-center, randomized controlled trial" and "the control group received a volume matched placebo infusion." Comment: Participants and treatment personnel were in a double-blind placebo-controlled trial, so knowledge of allocation was likely prevented. The outcomes assessed here (ECG and echocardiographic measures) are objective and unlikely to be influenced by participants/personnel knowing treatment assignment, supporting a low risk judgement overall.

Quote: "All ECGs were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment arm." and "All echocardiograms were interpreted at the central cardiology core lab by a cardiologist blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: The outcome assessor was an independent cardiologist at a central core lab and was explicitly blinded to treatment. The assessed outcomes (ECG and echocardiographic parameters such as ejection fraction, LV mass index, PR/QRS/QTc intervals) are objective measurements with standardized criteria, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

Quote: "Five (8%) patients in the enzyme replacement therapy group and four (14%) patients in the placebo group did not complete the study. Reasons for not completing the study in the treatment group included the following: hypersensitivity reactions related to the study medication (2), death unrelated to treatment (1), and switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (2). In the control group, the reasons included: adverse event not related to study medication (1), switch to commercial enzyme replacement therapy (1), and missed 78-week study appointments (2)." Comment: Attrition was modest overall (9/90 randomized, 10%) and reasonably balanced between groups, with reasons largely not obviously related to the cardiovascular outcomes assessed here. The paper also reports follow-up data for most participants and does not suggest differential missingness likely to bias the result.
